payload.goal: Add cache-aware authorization execution in the authorization service to reduce backend work while preserving the existing public authorization API and behavior.

acceptanceCriteria.statement: AuthorizationService.authorize must consult AuthorizationCache.get for a subject-derived key before performing permission checks against the backend.
On cache miss or empty snapshot, authorize must call backend.version and backend.loadPermissions for the subject and then persist both returned fields via cache.set.
When cache returns a non-stale snapshot for the same subject context, authorize must use cached permissions instead of calling backend.loadPermissions for that request.
When cached snapshot version is absent or does not match backend version, authorize must refresh the cache by reloading from backend and overwrite the snapshot.
All current authorization decision outcomes (allowed/denied, tenant scoping, invalid subject, backend error) must remain unchanged from previous behavior for valid inputs and failure paths.
Add or extend tests under test/authorization-service.test.ts to assert cache-miss, cache-hit, stale-cache refresh, and cache-write behavior with observable backend call counts and tenant isolation.

protectedInvariants.statement: No exported/public types or method signatures, including constructor and authorize, may be changed.
No new production dependencies may be added and no non-test tooling dependencies may be introduced in package manifests.
Behavior for authorization semantics and validation must remain compatible with current tests, including tenant separation rules.
Any modifications must stay confined to authorization service caching logic and its tests with no unrelated module behavior changes.
Project-wide strict TypeScript guarantees and existing strict compiler configuration semantics must remain intact.

nonGoals: Implement cache TTL, eviction policy, multi-level/distributed caching, or background refresh behavior.
Expose new public cache-management APIs or configuration surface area.
Add new dependency-backed observability, metrics, or tracing hooks unrelated to functional correctness of caching.
Modify build/test scripts, repository tooling, or unrelated package metadata.
Add any non-cache authentication, permission-model, or tenant-model features.
approvalRequiredChanges: Approve a concrete cache-keying strategy if it is not simply the subject identifier already implied by existing tenant-separation behavior.
Approve any deviation from a version-staleness policy for cache invalidation (for example, introducing TTL or probabilistic refresh).
Approve fallback semantics if cache failures should be propagated versus treated as backend fallback only.
evidenceGaps: There is no existing benchmark or assertion baseline proving before/after backend-call reduction for authorization flows.
No current test suite asserts cache correctness patterns, so exact edge-case call-order expectations are not yet evidenced.
The repository does not document cache fault-tolerance policy (soft-fail/fallback vs hard-fail) for cache read/write errors.
risks: An incorrect cache-key scope could cause cross-tenant permission leakage or incorrect denial/allow outcomes.
Version-skew or stale snapshot logic could serve outdated permissions if validation checks are incomplete.
Over-eager short-circuiting on cache hits could skip required backend validation and change existing authorization results.
unknowns: Whether the cache key should be raw subject, tenant-scoped subject, or another normalized variant is not specified by existing code or tests.
Whether cache transport failures should be ignored, logged, or surfaced is not currently defined.
Whether cache.set failures should abort authorization or be treated as non-fatal cache misses is not defined.
